corpus_building.implemented_as: The TXT paper is converted into a section-aware annotated mini-corpus.

tokenization.implemented_as: Every concept phrase and sentence is normalized into tokens for counts and n-grams.

normalization.implemented_as: Lower-casing and punctuation cleanup are used for phrase matching and deduplication.

n_gram.implemented_as: Concept length and document top unigrams/bigrams/trigrams are exposed.

bow_and_naive_bayes.implemented_as: Section BoW hits act as class-conditional section evidence for intro/method/experiment/conclusion style decisions.

tf_idf_document_term_matrix.implemented_as: Document-level frequency and TF-IDF matrices expose which section concepts are characteristic of this paper.

laplace_smoothing.implemented_as: P(section | concept) uses add-one smoothing, so unseen section-concept pairs are not forced to zero.

evidence_cue_lexicon.implemented_as: Cue phrases such as propose, achieve, outperform, and BLEU give transparent rule-based evidence hints.

sequence_labeling.implemented_as: The first model contributes BIO boundary evidence for concept phrase extraction.

canonicalization_noisy_channel_style.implemented_as: Observed surface phrases are mapped to canonical terms with an alias confidence score.

location.sentence: While we believe that in practice the increased computation time of multiple crops does not justify the potential gains in accuracy, for reference we also evaluate our networks using 50 crops per scale ( 5 × 5 regular grid with 2 flips), for a total of 150 crops over 3 scales, which is comparable to 144 crops over 4 scales used by Szegedy et al.

top_candidates.sentence: The top-1 error of the shallow net was measured to be 7% higher than that of B (on a center crop), which confirms that a deep net with small filters outperforms a shallow net with larger filters.
As can be seen, using multiple crops performs slightly better than dense evaluation, and the two approaches are indeed complementary, as their combination outperforms each of them.

It was demonstrated that the representation depth is beneficial for the classification accuracy, and that state-of-the-art performance on the ImageNet challenge dataset can be achieved using a conventional ConvNet architecture with substantially increased depth.
On the test set, the configuration E achieves 7.3% top-5 error.
In this section, we present the image classification results achieved by the described ConvNet architectures on the ILSVRC-2012 dataset (which was used for ILSVRC 2012-2014 challenges).
The dataset includes images of 1000 classes, and is split into three sets: training (1.3M images), validation (50K images), and testing (100K images with held-out class labels).
The classification performance is evaluated using two measures: the top-1 and top-5 error.
In this work we investigate the effect of the convolutional network depth on its accuracy in the large-scale image recognition setting.

Our main contribution is a thorough evaluation of networks of increasing depth using an architecture with very small (3 × 3) convolution filters, which shows that a significant improvement on the prior-art configurations can be achieved by pushing the depth to 16-19 weight layers.
These findings were the basis of our ImageNet Challenge 2014 submission, where our team secured the first and the second places in the localisation and classification tracks respectively.
We also show that our representations generalise well to other datasets, where they achieve state-of-the-art results.

In particular, an important role in the advance of deep visual recognition architectures has been played by the ImageNet Large-Scale Visual Recognition Challenge (ILSVRC) , which has served as a testbed for a few generations of large-scale image classification systems, from high-dimensional shallow feature encodings (the winner of to deep ConvNets (the winner of .
With ConvNets becoming more of a commodity in the computer vision field, a number of attempts have been made to improve the original architecture of in a bid to achieve better accuracy.
With ConvNets becoming more of a commodity in the computer vision field, a number of attempts have been made to improve the original architecture of in a bid to achieve better accuracy.
In this paper, we address another important aspect of ConvNet architecture design -its depth.
In this paper, we address another important aspect of ConvNet architecture design -its depth.

To this end, we fix other parameters of the architecture, and steadily increase the depth of the network by adding more convolutional layers, which is feasible due to the use of very small (3 × 3) convolution filters in all layers.

As a result, we come up with significantly more accurate ConvNet architectures, which not only achieve the state-of-the-art accuracy on ILSVRC classification and localisation tasks, but are also applicable to other image recognition datasets, where they achieve excellent performance even when used as a part of a relatively simple pipelines (e.g. deep features classified by a linear SVM without fine-tuning).

As a result, we come up with significantly more accurate ConvNet architectures, which not only achieve the state-of-the-art accuracy on ILSVRC classification and localisation tasks, but are also applicable to other image recognition datasets, where they achieve excellent performance even when used as a part of a relatively simple pipelines (e.g. deep features classified by a linear SVM without fine-tuning).

On a system equipped with four NVIDIA Titan Black GPUs, training a single net took 2-3 weeks depending on the architecture.
In this section, we present the image classification results achieved by the described ConvNet architectures on the ILSVRC-2012 dataset (which was used for ILSVRC 2012-2014 challenges).
The error rate of our architecture saturates when the depth reaches 19 layers, but even deeper models might be beneficial for larger datasets.
It should be noted that 1 × 1 conv. layers have recently been utilised in the "Network in Network" architecture of .

Small-size convolution filters have been previously used by , but their nets are significantly less deep than ours, and they did not evaluate on the large-scale ILSVRC dataset. applied deep ConvNets (11 weight layers) to the task of street number recognition, and showed that the increased depth led to better performance.
